German historian (1795–1886)

**Leopold von Ranke**[[a]](#cite_note-3) (21 December 1795 – 23 May 1886) was a German historian and a founder of modern [source](/wiki/Source_text "Source text")-based [history](/wiki/History "History").[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5) He was able to implement the [seminar teaching](/wiki/Seminar "Seminar") method in his classroom and focused on archival research and the analysis of historical documents. Building on the methods of the [Göttingen school of history](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen_school_of_history "Göttingen school of history"),[[5]](#cite_note-Ranke2010-6) he was the first to establish a historical seminar. Ranke set the standards for much of later historical writing, introducing such ideas as reliance on [primary sources](/wiki/Primary_source "Primary source") ([empiricism](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism")), an emphasis on [narrative history](/wiki/Narrative_history "Narrative history") and especially international politics (*[Außenpolitik](/wiki/Political_history "Political history")*). He was ennobled in 1865, with the addition of a "[von](/wiki/Von "Von")" to his name.

Ranke also had a great influence on Western historiography and is considered a symbol of the quality of 19th century German historical studies. Ranke, influenced by [Barthold Georg Niebuhr](/wiki/Barthold_Georg_Niebuhr "Barthold Georg Niebuhr"), was very talented in constructing narratives without exceeding the limits of historical evidence. His critics have noted the influence of [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") in guiding his work, especially his belief that God's actions were manifest in the lives of men and history, a viewpoint that shaped his ideas that the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") was a manifestation of God's intent.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leopold_von_Ranke&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Ranke was born in [Wiehe](/wiki/Wiehe "Wiehe"), [Thuringia](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia"), [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony"). Wiehe was then a part of the [Electorate of Saxony](/wiki/Electorate_of_Saxony "Electorate of Saxony").[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-8) He came from a family of Lutheran pastors and lawyers. He was educated partly at home and partly in the [high school](/wiki/Gymnasium_(Germany) "Gymnasium (Germany)") at [Schulpforta](/wiki/Schulpforta "Schulpforta"). His early years engendered a lifelong love of [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"), [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"). In 1814, Ranke entered [Leipzig University](/wiki/Leipzig_University "Leipzig University"),[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-8) where his subjects were [Classics](/wiki/Classics "Classics") and Lutheran [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"). At [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig"), Ranke became an expert in [philology](/wiki/Philology "Philology") and translation of the ancient authors into [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"). His teachers included [Johann Gottfried Jakob Hermann](/wiki/Johann_Gottfried_Jakob_Hermann "Johann Gottfried Jakob Hermann"). As a student, Ranke's favorite authors were [Thucydides](/wiki/Thucydides "Thucydides"), [Livy](/wiki/Livy "Livy"), [Dionysius of Halicarnassus](/wiki/Dionysius_of_Halicarnassus "Dionysius of Halicarnassus"), [Johann Wolfgang von Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe"), [Barthold Georg Niebuhr](/wiki/Barthold_Georg_Niebuhr "Barthold Georg Niebuhr"), [Immanuel Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant"), [Johann Gottlieb Fichte](/wiki/Johann_Gottlieb_Fichte "Johann Gottlieb Fichte"), [Friedrich Wilhelm Joseph Schelling](/wiki/Friedrich_Wilhelm_Joseph_Schelling "Friedrich Wilhelm Joseph Schelling") and [Friedrich Schlegel](/wiki/Friedrich_Schlegel "Friedrich Schlegel"). Ranke showed little interest in the work of modern history because of his dissatisfaction with what he regarded as history books that were merely a collection of facts lumped together by modern historians.

Between 1817 and 1825, Ranke worked as a schoolmaster teaching classics at the Friedrichs Gymnasium in [Frankfurt an der Oder](/wiki/Frankfurt_an_der_Oder "Frankfurt an der Oder"). During this time, he became interested in [history](/wiki/History "History") in part because of his desire to be involved in the developing field of a more professionalized history and in part because of his desire to find the hand of [God](/wiki/God "God") in the workings of history.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leopold_von_Ranke&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

In 1824, Ranke launched his career with the book *Geschichten der romanischen und germanischen Völker von 1494 bis 1514*[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-8) (*Histories of the Latin and Teutonic Peoples from 1494 to 1514*) in which he used an unusually wide variety of sources for a historian of the age, including "memoirs, diaries, personal and formal missives, government documents, diplomatic dispatches and first-hand accounts of eye-witnesses". In that sense, he leaned on the traditions of philology but emphasized mundane documents instead of old and exotic literature.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

After the minister of education was impressed with the work of a historian who did not have access to the nation's great public libraries, Ranke was given a position in the [University of Berlin](/wiki/University_of_Berlin "University of Berlin"), where he was a professor for nearly fifty years, starting in 1825. At the university, he used the seminar system and taught how to check the value of sources. Ranke became deeply involved in the dispute between the followers of the legal professor [Friedrich Carl von Savigny](/wiki/Friedrich_Carl_von_Savigny "Friedrich Carl von Savigny"), who emphasized the varieties of different periods of history, and the followers of the philosopher [Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel](/wiki/Georg_Wilhelm_Friedrich_Hegel "Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel"), who saw history as the unfolding of a universal story. Ranke supported Savigny and criticized the Hegelian view of history as being a one-size-fits-all approach. Also during his time in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), Ranke became the first historian to use the forty-seven volumes that comprised the diplomatic archives of [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice") from the 16th and 17th centuries. Since many archives opened up during this time, he sent out his students to these places to recruit information. In his classrooms, he would discuss the sources that his students would find and would emphasize that history should be told "as it really happened" (*wie es eigentlich gewesen*). Therefore, he is often seen as "the pioneer of a critical historical science".[[10]](#cite_note-11) Meanwhile, Ranke came to prefer dealing with primary sources as opposed to secondary sources.

It was in Vienna where the friendship of [Friedrich von Gentz](/wiki/Friedrich_von_Gentz "Friedrich von Gentz") and the protection of [Klemens von Metternich](/wiki/Klemens_von_Metternich "Klemens von Metternich") opened to him the Venetian Archives, a fresh source, the value of which he first discovered;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] it is still not exhausted.[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-8) He found time to write a short book entitled *Die Serbische Revolution* (1829)[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-8) from material supplied to him by [Vuk Karadžić](/wiki/Vuk_Karad%C5%BEi%C4%87 "Vuk Karadžić"), a Serb who had himself been witness to the scenes he related during the [First Serbian Uprising](/wiki/First_Serbian_Uprising "First Serbian Uprising") in 1804. This was afterwards expanded into *Serbien und die Turkei im 19 Jahrhundert* (1879).

At the behest of the Prussian government, Ranke founded and edited the *Historische-Politische Zeitschrift* journal from 1832 to 1836. Ranke, who was a [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism"), used the journal to attack the ideas of [liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism"). In his 1833 article "The Great Powers" and his 1836 article "Dialogue on Politics", Ranke claimed that every state is given a special moral character from God and individuals should strive best to fulfill the "idea" of their state. Thus, in this way, Ranke urged his readers to stay loyal to the Prussian state and to reject the ideas of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), which Ranke claimed were meant for France only.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

From 1834 to 1836, Ranke published *Die römischen Päpste, ihre Kirche und ihr Staat im sechzehnten und siebzehnten Jahrhundert*[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-8) (*The Popes of Rome, Their Church and State in the Sixteenth and Seventeenth Centuries*) (3 vols.). As a Protestant, Ranke was barred from viewing the [Vatican Secret Archive](/wiki/Vatican_Apostolic_Archive "Vatican Apostolic Archive") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), but based on private papers in Rome and Venice, he was able to explain the history of the [papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy") in the 16th century.[[12]](#cite_note-13) In this book, Ranke coined the term "[Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation")" and offered colorful portrayals of [Pope Paul IV](/wiki/Pope_Paul_IV "Pope Paul IV"), [Ignatius of Loyola](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Loyola "Ignatius of Loyola") and [Pope Pius V](/wiki/Pope_Pius_V "Pope Pius V"). He promoted research into primary sources: "I see the time approaching when we shall base modern history, no longer on the reports even of contemporary historians, except insofar as they had personal and immediate knowledge of facts; and still less on work yet more remote from the source; but rather on the narratives of eyewitnesses, and on genuine and original documents".[[13]](#cite_note-14)

The papacy denounced Ranke's book as anti-Catholic. In contrast, many Protestants denounced it as not anti-Catholic enough. Still, historians have generally praised him for placing the situation of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") in the context of the 16th century and for his fair treatment of the complex interaction of the political and religious issues in that century. The British Catholic historian [Lord Acton](/wiki/John_Dalberg-Acton,_1st_Baron_Acton "John Dalberg-Acton, 1st Baron Acton") defended Ranke's book as the most fair-minded, balanced and objective study ever written on the papacy of the 16th century.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

In 1841, his fame in its ascendancy, Ranke was appointed [Historiographer Royal](/wiki/Historiographer_Royal "Historiographer Royal") to the Prussian court. In 1845, he became a member of the [Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Royal_Netherlands_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences").[[15]](#cite_note-16)

In Paris, Ranke met the Irish woman [Clarissa Helena Graves](/wiki/Clarissa_von_Ranke "Clarissa von Ranke") (born 1808) from Dublin in July 1843. She had been educated in England and the Continent. They were engaged on 1 October and married in [Bowness](/wiki/Bowness-on-Windermere "Bowness-on-Windermere"), England in a ceremony officiated by her brother [Robert Perceval Graves](/wiki/Robert_Perceval_Graves "Robert Perceval Graves"), an Anglican priest.[[16]](#cite_note-17) They had three sons (one of whom died in infancy), and one daughter.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

From 1847 to 1848, Ranke published *Neun Bücher preußischer Geschichte* (translated as *Memoirs of the House of Brandenburg and History of Prussia, during the Seventeenth and Eighteenth Centuries*)[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-8) in which he examined the fortunes of the [Hohenzollern family](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern") and state from the Middle Ages to the reign of [Frederick the Great](/wiki/Frederick_the_Great "Frederick the Great"). Many Prussian nationalists were offended by Ranke's portrayal of Prussia as a typical medium-sized German state rather than as a great power. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From 1852 to 1861, Ranke published *French History Mainly in the 16th and 17th Centuries* (5 vols.), covering [Francis I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France") to [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") and gaining him more praise for his impartiality despite being German.

In a series of lectures[[18]](#cite_note-19) given before future King [Maximilian II of Bavaria](/wiki/Maximilian_II_of_Bavaria "Maximilian II of Bavaria") in 1854, Ranke argued that "every age is next to God", by which he meant that every period of history is unique and must be understood in its own context. He argued that God gazes over history in its totality and finds all periods equal. Ranke rejected the teleological approach to history, by which each period is considered inferior to the following period. Thus, the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") were not inferior to the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), simply different. In Ranke's view, historians had to understand a period and its terms and seek to find only the general ideas that animated every period of history. For Ranke, history was not to be an account of man's "progress" because "[a]fter Plato, there can be no more Plato". Ultimately, "[h]istory is no criminal court".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For Ranke, Christianity was morally most superior and could not be improved upon. When he wrote *Zur orientalischen Frage. Gutachten* at the behest of the Kaiser he framed the conflict with the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") as primarily religious; the civil rights of Christians against Muslims in the Ottoman Empire could only be secured by the intervention of the Christian European nations.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

From 1854 to 1857, Ranke published *History of the Reformation in Germany* (*Deutsche Geschichte im Zeitalter der Reformation*),[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-8) using the ninety-six volumes of correspondence from ambassadors to the Imperial Diet he found in [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt") to explain the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") as the result of both politics and religion.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From 1859 to 1867, Ranke published the six-volume *History of England Principally in the Sixteenth and Seventeenth Centuries* (*Englische Geschichte vornehmlich im XVI und XVII Jahrhundert*), followed by an expanded nine-volume edition from 1870 to 1884, which extended his huge reach even further. At this point, he was eighty years old, and devoted the rest of his career to shorter treatises on German history that supplement his earlier writings.

## Later life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leopold_von_Ranke&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Later life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Leopold_Von_Ranke_1877.jpg/250px-Leopold_Von_Ranke_1877.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leopold_Von_Ranke_1877.jpg)

1877 portrait of Ranke

The honors poured in when Ranke was ennobled in 1865, appointed a Prussian Privy Councillor in 1882 and given an honorary citizenship of Berlin in 1885. In 1884, he was appointed the first honorary member of the [American Historical Association](/wiki/American_Historical_Association "American Historical Association"). In 1885, he was elected as a member to the [American Philosophical Society](/wiki/American_Philosophical_Society "American Philosophical Society").[[20]](#cite_note-21) After his retirement in 1871, Ranke continued to write on a variety of subjects relating to [German history](/wiki/History_of_Germany "History of Germany") such as the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars"), [Albrecht von Wallenstein](/wiki/Albrecht_von_Wallenstein "Albrecht von Wallenstein"), [Karl August von Hardenberg](/wiki/Karl_August_von_Hardenberg "Karl August von Hardenberg"),[[21]](#cite_note-22) and King [Frederick William IV of Prussia](/wiki/Frederick_William_IV_of_Prussia "Frederick William IV of Prussia"). In 1880, Ranke began a huge six-volume work on [world history](/wiki/World_history_(field) "World history (field)") which began with ancient [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") and the [Israelites](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites"). By the time of his death in Berlin in 1886 at the age of 90, Ranke had reached only the 12th century, but his assistants later used his notes to take the series up to 1453.
After his wife died in 1871, Ranke became half-blind, depending on assistants to read to him. A diary entry from January 1877 contains his mature thoughts about being a historian:[[22]](#cite_note-23)

> The proverb tells us that poets are born. Not only in the arts, but even in some scholarly fields, young men develop into full bloom, or at least display their originality. Musicians and mathematicians have the expectation of attaining eminence in early years. But a historian must be old, not only because of the immeasurable extent of his field of study, but because of the insight into the historical process which a long life confers, especially under changing conditions. It would hardly be bearable for him to have only a short span of experience. For his personal development requires that great events complete their course before his eyes, that others collapse, that new forms be attempted.

After Ranke's death, [Syracuse University](/wiki/Syracuse_University "Syracuse University") purchased his collection. The [Ranke Library](/wiki/Ranke_Library "Ranke Library") of 25,000 books and other materials was ten times as large as the university's own.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

## Methodology and criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leopold_von_Ranke&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Methodology and criticism")]

At the core of his method, Ranke did not believe that general theories could cut across time and space. Instead, he made statements about the time using quotations from primary sources, saying: "My understanding of 'leading ideas' is simply that they are the dominant tendencies in each century. However, these tendencies can be only described; they cannot, in the last resort, be summed up in a concept". Ranke objected to [philosophy of history](/wiki/Philosophy_of_history "Philosophy of history"), particularly as practiced by Hegel, claiming that Hegel ignored the role of human agency in history which was too essential to be "characterized through only one idea or one word" or "circumscribed by a concept".[[24]](#cite_note-25) This lack of emphasis on unifying theories or themes led [Rudolf Haym](/wiki/Rudolf_Haym "Rudolf Haym") to denigrate his ideas as "the mindlessness of the empiricist".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the 19th century, Ranke's work was very popular and his ideas about historical practice gradually became dominant in western historiography. However, he had critics among his contemporaries, including [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx"), a former Hegelian, who suggested that Ranke engaged in some of the practices he criticized in other historians.

Ranke began his first book with the statement in the introduction that he would show the unity of the experiences of the "Teutonic" nations of [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), [England](/wiki/England "England") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and the "Latin" nations of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and [France](/wiki/France "France") through the great "respirations" of the [*Völkerwanderung*](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period") (great migration), the [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") and colonization that in Ranke's view bound all of the nations together to produce modern European civilization. Despite his opening statement, Ranke largely treated all of the nations under examination separately until the outbreak of the wars for the control of Italy starting in 1494. However, the book is best remembered for Ranke's comment: "To history has been assigned the office of judging the past, of instructing the present for the benefit of future ages. To such high offices this work does not aspire: It wants only to show what actually happened (*wie es eigentlich gewesen*)".[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) Ranke's statement that history should embrace the principle of *wie es eigentlich gewesen* (meaning "how things actually were") was subsequently taken by many historians as their guiding principle. There has been much debate over the precise meaning of this phrase. Some[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] have argued that adhering to the principle of *wie es eigentlich gewesen* means that the historian should document facts, but not offer any interpretation of these facts. Following [Georg Iggers](/wiki/Georg_Iggers "Georg Iggers"), [Peter Novick](/wiki/Peter_Novick "Peter Novick") has argued that Ranke, who was more of a romantic and idealist than his American contemporaries understood, meant instead that the historian should discover the facts and find the essences behind them. Under this view, the word *eigentlich* should be translated as "essentially", the aim then being to "show what essentially happened".[[27]](#cite_note-28) Ranke went on to write that the historian must seek the "Holy hieroglyph" that is God's hand in history, keeping an "eye for the universal" whilst taking "pleasure in the particular".[[28]](#cite_note-29)

While Ranke's methods remain influential in the practice of history, his broader ideas of [historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography") and empiricism are now regarded by some as outdated and no longer credible. They held sway among historians until the mid-20th century, when they were challenged by [E. H. Carr](/wiki/E._H._Carr "E. H. Carr") and [Fernand Braudel](/wiki/Fernand_Braudel "Fernand Braudel"). Carr opposed Ranke's ideas of empiricism as naive, boring and outmoded, saying that historians did not merely report facts; they choose which facts they use. Braudel's approach was based on the *histoire problème*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Remarking on the legacy of Ranke's dictum that historians should represent the past *wie es eigentlich gewesen* ("as it actually was"),[[29]](#cite_note-30) [Walter Benjamin](/wiki/Walter_Benjamin "Walter Benjamin") scathingly wrote that it represented "the strongest narcotic of the [19th] century".[[30]](#cite_note-31)
